contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: For one query, compare the entire legal strategy cost—including sorting, copying, predicate work, and access model—rather than comparing O(log n) with O(n) in isolation.

items.prompt: A random-access array is already sorted and receives one exact query. Both implementations are correct. Which asymptotic comparison applies?

options.text: Binary search is O(log n) and a worst-case scan is O(n).
Both are O(n).
The scan is O(1) because it may find target first.
Any midpoint loop is O(log n).

feedback.reason: When sorted order and random access already exist, binary search has the lower worst-case query cost.
feedback.details: The one-query contrast changes if sorting, copying, or expensive predicate work must be added first.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_linear: A legal binary search halves the remaining interval.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_constant: Worst-case analysis includes target absent or at the end.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_without_invariant: The statement is true only for a correct half-discard implementation.

items.prompt: An unsorted random-access array receives one exact query. Which complete asymptotic comparison is correct?

options.text: A scan is O(n); sorting solely to enable binary search is O(n log n) overall.
Binary search is O(log n) on the unsorted array.
Sort plus binary search is O(log n) because only query time matters.
Both are O(log n).

feedback.reason: One-query strategy comparison includes the cost required to make each strategy legal.
feedback.details: A direct scan is both correct and asymptotically cheaper than one-off sorting.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_log: The method is not correct without ordering.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_log: A scan can inspect n positions.

items.prompt: A sorted array has n = 6. Both a scan and binary search are correct. Which conclusion is defensible?

options.text: Binary search has better asymptotic worst case, but simplicity and measured constants may justify a scan for this tiny fixed size.
Binary search is mandatory because O(log n) always dominates engineering considerations.
The scan has the better asymptotic class.
Only one of the two is correct.

feedback.reason: Asymptotic advantage and practical preference are related but not identical.
feedback.details: The transfer boundary is growth: binary search matters as n scales, while tiny fixed domains may favor simpler code.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_mandatory: Asymptotics do not determine every fixed-size implementation decision.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_asymptotically_better: O(n) is worse than O(log n) as n grows.

items.prompt: A scan stops at the first element and binary search takes several iterations. Does that make the scan asymptotically better for one successful input?

options.text: No. That is a favorable case for the scan, not its worst-case growth bound.
Yes. Linear scan is O(1) because one input finishes immediately.
Yes, and binary search is therefore incorrect.
Both always perform the same number of comparisons.

feedback.reason: Single-query cost comparison should distinguish best case, expected case, and worst case.
feedback.details: A scan can be excellent on early hits while retaining O(n) worst-case behavior.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_o1: Big-O worst case is not determined by one instance.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_wrong: Iteration count does not imply incorrectness.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_always: Their traces vary by target location and absence.

items.prompt: A sorted linked list receives one exact query. Which total-cost comparison is most accurate without extra indexing?

options.text: A sequential scan is O(n); repeatedly locating midpoints does not give ordinary O(log n) array-style binary search.
Binary search is automatically O(log n) because the values are sorted.
The scan is O(log n) because the list is sorted.
Both use O(1) extra space, so both are O(1) time.

feedback.reason: Data access cost is part of the strategy cost model.
feedback.details: Binary search becomes attractive after materializing or maintaining a random-access index, which is preprocessing.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_log: Sequential traversal remains linear in the worst case.

items.prompt: A sorted array query asks whether any value satisfies an arbitrary O(1) condition unrelated to the sort key. Which one-query method is correct?

options.text: Scan until a satisfying value is found or the array ends.
Binary-search the arbitrary condition because each check is O(1).
Check only the midpoint.
Sort again by the same key.

feedback.reason: For one query, correctness still precedes cost comparison.
feedback.details: A separate index ordered by the queried property could move the boundary, but the existing sort does not.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary: Cheap evaluation does not make the truth pattern monotonic.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_only: Other positions may satisfy the condition.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_again: The condition remains unrelated to that order.

items.prompt: A monotonic predicate search over W candidates calls an O(n) feasibility function. A direct O(n) algorithm solves the same one-query problem. Which is asymptotically cheaper?

options.text: The direct O(n) method, compared with O(n log W) for repeated predicate evaluation.
Binary search because it uses only O(log W) candidates.
They are always equal.
The result depends on the midpoint variable name.

feedback.reason: The full cost of one query includes every predicate evaluation.
feedback.details: Binary search saves candidate evaluations, but it may repeat expensive global work that a direct algorithm performs once.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary: Each candidate costs O(n) to evaluate.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.equal: The binary strategy has an additional logarithmic factor.

items.prompt: An immutable unsorted input must remain in original order, but an O(n) additional copy is allowed. For one query, which strategy is asymptotically preferable?

options.text: Scan the original in O(n).
Copy, sort, and binary-search in O(n log n).
Sort the original in place.
Binary-search the unsorted original.

feedback.reason: Allowing a copy makes preprocessing legal but not necessarily economical.
feedback.details: For one query, the scan avoids both O(n) extra storage and O(n log n) sorting work.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.copy_sort_binary: The one-query setup is asymptotically larger.

items.prompt: A sorted array query requires returning every occurrence of target. Which one-query cost statement is correct if k matches are output?

options.text: Boundary searches can locate the range in O(log n), but materializing k results still costs Ω(k).
The entire operation is O(log n) regardless of k.
A scan is O(n) and output has no additional cost.
Return one midpoint match because binary search is faster.

feedback.reason: A faster search phase does not remove output-sensitive lower bounds.
feedback.details: Binary search can still beat a full scan when k is small, but total time includes the emitted range.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.pure_log: Required output work cannot be skipped.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_always_n_only: Writing k results is part of the work and output space.

items.prompt: A sorted array is stored remotely. Each random access has high network latency, while one sequential streaming pass is efficient. Which decision is justified?

options.text: Compare the real access model; a sequential scan may beat logarithmically many expensive random accesses.
Binary search is always faster because it makes fewer comparisons.
Scanning is always faster on remote data.
Use asymptotic classes without considering operation costs.

feedback.reason: Asymptotic operation counts assume a cost model for each access.
feedback.details: When random and sequential accesses have radically different costs, one-query strategy may differ from the RAM model.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_always: Comparison count is not the complete access cost.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_always: Cached indexes or batch endpoints can change the model.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_latency: The cost model must reflect material operations.

items.prompt: Which terms belong in a complete one-query comparison when data is initially unsorted? Select all that apply.

options.text: The cost of directly scanning the input.
Any copying and sorting needed to create a legal binary-search representation.
The binary-search query cost after preprocessing.
Benefits from unspecified future queries that are not part of the workload.

feedback.reason: A single-query model contains no automatic amortization over imagined future work.
feedback.details: The entire legal path to the answer must be counted.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.future_queries: The comparison should not assume reuse that the contract does not provide.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.query_cost: It remains part of the total.

items.prompt: Which conditions favor binary search for one query? Select all that apply.

options.text: The data is already sorted by the query key and supports random access.
A cheap predicate is proven monotonic over a large ordered candidate range.
n is large enough that the logarithmic reduction matters under the actual cost model.
The data is unsorted and the query condition is arbitrary.

feedback.reason: One-query binary search needs both legal structure and a workload where the reduced checks matter.
feedback.details: Unsorted arbitrary conditions stay on the scan side of the boundary.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.already_sorted_random: No setup is required and half elimination is legal.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.large_n: Scale can make the asymptotic advantage material.

items.prompt: Which conditions favor a one-time scan? Select all that apply.

options.text: One query over unsorted data with no existing index.
The query condition is nonmonotonic in the available order.
The representation offers efficient sequential access but expensive midpoint access.
A large random-access array is already sorted by the query key.

feedback.reason: Scanning can win through lower setup cost, stronger correctness, or a better access pattern.
feedback.details: Existing aligned random-access order shifts the choice toward binary search.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.stable_sorted_large: This is the canonical binary-search case.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sequential_access: The physical access model favors scanning.

items.prompt: Which distinctions should be stated in a one-query performance claim? Select all that apply.

options.text: Best-case versus worst-case behavior.
Whether ordered data or an index already exists.
Random-access, comparison, and predicate costs.
Whether the implementation uses standard variable names.

feedback.reason: A meaningful comparison states the workload and cost model.
feedback.details: Otherwise “binary is faster” collapses several distinct cases into a slogan.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.best_worst: Early scan success differs from absent-target cost.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.access_cost: The unit-cost RAM model may not apply.

items.prompt: Arrange the decision process for one exact query.

elements.text: State whether data is ordered, mutable, random-access, and query-aligned.
Discard strategies that cannot satisfy the result contract.
For each legal strategy, sum setup, access, predicate, query, and output work.
Choose the lower-cost or simpler legal strategy for this one query.

feedback.reason: Single-query analysis has no hidden future workload to amortize setup.
feedback.details: The decision follows complete cost after correctness filtering.

items.prompt: Arrange the analysis of an early-exit scan versus binary search.

elements.text: State whether the claim concerns best, average, or worst case.
Count scan inspections under that case distribution.
Count legal binary-search iterations and access costs.
Compare both strategies under the same case and cost model.

feedback.reason: Comparing the scan best case with binary-search worst case is not a valid general conclusion.
feedback.details: Both strategies must be evaluated under aligned assumptions.

items.prompt: One exact query is made on an unsorted array. Strategy A scans. Strategy B copies, sorts, and binary-searches. Select total time and additional space.
constraints: Comparison sorting is O(n log n).
The sorted copy occupies O(n) space.

feedback.reason: The one-query binary strategy pays setup in both time and memory.
feedback.details: The scan needs no additional ordered representation.

items.prompt: A sorted random-access array receives one exact query. Select worst-case time and auxiliary space for iterative binary search and linear scan.
constraints: Neither materializes output beyond one index or boolean.

feedback.reason: Existing legal order exposes the pure query-cost contrast.
feedback.details: Both iterative implementations use constant extra state.

items.prompt: A monotonic candidate search has range width W and predicate cost P(n). A direct algorithm answers the same query in D(n). Select the binary-search total and the comparison rule.
constraints: The binary search performs O(log W) predicate evaluations.

feedback.reason: The one-query choice depends on full algorithmic work, not the outer loop alone.
feedback.details: A direct solution can beat a legal binary search when it avoids repeated predicate computation.

items.prompt: A sorted dataset is streamed sequentially from remote storage. One sequential pass costs S(n), while each random midpoint request costs L and binary search makes O(log n) such requests. Select the two cost expressions.
constraints: Ignore local computation relative to access cost.

feedback.reason: The preferred strategy depends on whether S(n) is smaller or larger than L log n.
feedback.details: Comparison count alone is insufficient when operation costs differ substantially.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: For one query, compare the entire legal strategy cost—including sorting, copying, predicate work, and access model—rather than comparing O(log n) with O(n) in isolation.
